proficiencyLevels.name: Absolute Beginner
proficiencyLevels.description: Start from zero — learn the alphabet, basic greetings, and survival phrases

sections.title: Everyday Basics
sections.description: Learn essential words and phrases for daily life

units.title: Greetings & Introductions
objectives: Say hello and goodbye in formal and informal settings
Introduce yourself with your name
Ask someone their name

newVocabulary.exampleSentence: Guten Morgen! Wie geht es Ihnen?

units.grammarFocus: Basic sentence structure: Subject + Verb (Ich heiße ...)
units.culturalNote: Germans distinguish between formal (Sie) and informal (du) address. Use 'Sie' with strangers and 'du' with friends.

exercises.instruction: Listen and read the sentence. Tap each word to see its meaning.

content.sentence: Guten Tag, ich heiße Anna.
content.translation: Good day, my name is Anna.

exercises.instruction: Complete the conversation by choosing the correct response.

dialogue.speaker: app
dialogue.translation: Good day! What is your name?

exercises.instruction: Match the German words with their English meanings.
Arrange the words to form a correct sentence.

content.translation: My name is Anna

exercises.instruction: Fill in the blank with the correct word.
exercises.hint: Think about how to say 'my name is' in German.

content.context: Introducing yourself

content.question: What does 'Guten Tag' mean?

content.rule: The verb 'heißen' conjugates to 'heiße' for 'ich' (I).
content.question: Ich _____ Thomas. (My name is Thomas.)
content.explanation: 'Heißen' conjugates to 'heiße' with the subject 'ich'.

exercises.instruction: Read the German text and write what it means in English.

content.prompt: Read the following and write what it means in English:
content.text: Hallo! Ich heiße Maria. Guten Tag!
content.sampleAnswer: Hello! My name is Maria. Good day!

units.title: Numbers 1-20
objectives: Count from 1 to 20 in German
Recognize written and spoken numbers
Use numbers in simple sentences
units.grammarFocus: German number words and their pronunciation patterns
units.culturalNote: Germans count on their fingers starting with the thumb for 'one'.

exercises.instruction: Listen and read the numbers. Tap each word to see its meaning.

content.sentence: Eins, zwei, drei, vier, fünf
content.translation: One, two, three, four, five

exercises.instruction: Complete the conversation by choosing the correct response.

dialogue.speaker: app
dialogue.translation: How many siblings do you have?

exercises.instruction: Match the German numbers with their English equivalents.
Arrange the words to form a correct sentence.

content.translation: I have two siblings

exercises.instruction: Fill in the blank with the correct number word.
exercises.hint: The number in parentheses is your clue.

content.context: Talking about family

content.question: What number is 'fünf'?

content.rule: In German, 'haben' (to have) conjugates to 'habe' for 'ich'.
content.question: Ich _____ drei Katzen. (I have three cats.)
content.explanation: 'Haben' conjugates to 'habe' with the subject 'ich'.

exercises.instruction: Read the German text and write what it means in English.

content.prompt: Read the following and write what it means:
content.text: Ich habe fünf Bücher und drei Stifte.
content.sampleAnswer: I have five books and three pens.

units.title: Colors & Descriptions
objectives: Name basic colors in German
Use colors as adjectives
Describe objects using colors
units.grammarFocus: Adjective placement: predicative (Das Auto ist rot) vs. attributive (das rote Auto)

exercises.instruction: Listen and read. Tap each word to see its meaning.

content.sentence: Das Auto ist rot.
content.translation: The car is red.

exercises.instruction: Match the German colors with their English meanings.
Arrange the words to form a correct sentence.

content.translation: The flower is yellow

exercises.instruction: Fill in the blank with the correct color.

content.context: Describing the sky

content.question: What color is 'grün'?

content.rule: In predicative use, German adjectives do not take endings: 'Das Auto ist rot.'
content.question: Das Haus ist _____. (The house is white.)
content.explanation: Predicative adjectives (after 'ist') take no ending in German.

exercises.instruction: Read the German text and write what it means in English.

content.text: Die Katze ist schwarz und weiß.
content.sampleAnswer: The cat is black and white.

units.title: The Alphabet & Pronunciation
objectives: Pronounce all German letters including umlauts (ä, ö, ü) and ß
Spell your name in German
Recognize special German sounds
units.grammarFocus: German umlauts (ä, ö, ü) and the Eszett (ß) — sounds that don't exist in English
units.culturalNote: In Germany, when spelling on the phone, people use city names: A wie Anton, B wie Berlin.

exercises.instruction: Listen to the German alphabet sounds.

content.sentence: A, B, C, D, E, F, G
content.translation: A, B, C, D, E, F, G

exercises.instruction: Match the special characters with their sounds.

content.prompt: A sharp 's' sound, used in words like 'Straße'

content.translation: Hello

exercises.instruction: Fill in the missing umlaut letter.

content.text: Ich m_____chte Kaffee. (I would like coffee.)

content.question: Which of these is NOT a German umlaut?

content.rule: The Eszett (ß) is used after long vowels. 'ss' is used after short vowels.
content.question: Die Stra___e ist lang. (The street is long.)
content.explanation: After the long 'a' sound in 'Straße', we use 'ß'.

content.prompt: Write the German word for 'street' using the correct special character:
content.sampleAnswer: Straße

units.title: Basic Phrases & Politeness
objectives: Use common polite phrases (please, thank you, excuse me)
Ask 'how are you?' and respond
Handle basic social interactions
units.grammarFocus: Formal (Sie) vs. informal (du) forms of address

content.sentence: Entschuldigung, wie geht es Ihnen?
content.translation: Excuse me, how are you?
I'm fine

content.context: Asking how someone is (formal)

content.question: What does 'Entschuldigung' mean?

content.rule: 'Wie geht es Ihnen?' uses formal 'Sie'. 'Wie geht es dir?' uses informal 'du'.
content.question: Wie geht es _____? (formal: How are you?)
content.explanation: 'Ihnen' is the formal dative form of 'Sie'.

content.text: Entschuldigung! Wie geht es Ihnen? Mir geht es gut, danke.
content.sampleAnswer: Excuse me! How are you? I'm fine, thank you.

units.title: Food & Drink Basics
objectives: Name common food and drink items
Say what you like and dislike
Order simple items
units.grammarFocus: Definite articles by gender: der (m), die (f), das (n)
units.culturalNote: Germans traditionally eat bread (Brot) for breakfast and dinner. Lunch is the main hot meal.

content.sentence: Kaffee und Milch, bitte.
content.translation: Coffee and milk, please.

dialogue.speaker: app
dialogue.translation: What would you like to drink?

content.translation: I would like water please

content.context: Ordering coffee

content.question: Which article goes with 'Milch'?

content.rule: German nouns have grammatical gender: der (masculine), die (feminine), das (neuter).
content.question: _____ Kaffee ist heiß. (The coffee is hot.)
content.explanation: 'Kaffee' is masculine, so it uses 'der'.

content.text: Ich möchte Kaffee und Brot, bitte.
content.sampleAnswer: I would like coffee and bread, please.

units.title: Family Members
objectives: Name immediate family members
Talk about your family
Use possessive pronouns (mein/meine)
units.grammarFocus: Possessive pronoun 'mein' changes with gender: mein Vater (m), meine Mutter (f), mein Kind (n)

content.sentence: Das ist meine Familie.
content.translation: This is my family.
My sister is called Anna

content.context: Talking about your brother

content.question: What does 'Schwester' mean?

content.rule: 'Mein' changes based on gender: mein (m/n), meine (f/pl).
content.question: _____ Schwester ist nett. (My sister is nice.)
content.explanation: 'Schwester' is feminine, so we use 'meine'.

content.text: Meine Familie ist groß. Ich habe zwei Brüder und eine Schwester.
content.sampleAnswer: My family is big. I have two brothers and one sister.

units.title: Animals
objectives: Name common animals
Say which animals you like
Use plural forms
units.grammarFocus: Plural formation patterns: -e (Hunde), -en (Katzen), umlaut+e (Vögel)

content.sentence: Ich habe einen Hund und eine Katze.
content.translation: I have a dog and a cat.
I have a cat

content.context: Talking about your pet dog

content.question: What is the plural of 'Tier'?

content.rule: In the accusative case, masculine articles change: der → den, ein → einen.
content.question: Ich habe _____ Hund. (I have a dog.)
content.explanation: 'Hund' is masculine. In the accusative, 'ein' becomes 'einen'.

content.text: Ich habe zwei Hunde und drei Fische.
content.sampleAnswer: I have two dogs and three fish.

units.title: Body Parts
objectives: Name basic body parts
Describe pain or discomfort simply
Use definite articles with body parts
units.grammarFocus: Body parts use definite articles, not possessives: 'Mir tut der Kopf weh' (My head hurts)

content.sentence: Das ist mein Kopf.
content.translation: This is my head.
My head hurts

content.question: What does 'Auge' mean?

content.rule: German uses definite articles (not possessives) with body parts in pain expressions.
content.question: Mir tut _____ Kopf weh.
content.explanation: German uses 'der Kopf' with the dative 'mir' instead of 'mein Kopf' in pain expressions.

content.text: Mir tut der Kopf weh und meine Hand tut auch weh.
content.sampleAnswer: My head hurts and my hand also hurts.

units.title: Review & Practice
objectives: Review all vocabulary from Section 1
Practice greetings, numbers, colors, and family
Build confidence with mixed exercises
units.grammarFocus: Review: articles (der/die/das), possessives (mein/meine), verb conjugation (ich heiße, ich habe)

content.sentence: Hallo! Ich heiße Maria. Ich habe zwei Katzen.
content.translation: Hello! My name is Maria. I have two cats.

dialogue.speaker: app
dialogue.translation: Good day! What is your name?

dialogue.speaker: app
dialogue.text: Freut mich! Wie geht es Ihnen?
dialogue.translation: Nice to meet you! How are you?

options.text: Ich heiße Thomas. / Mir geht es gut, danke!

exercises.instruction: Match all the words you've learned.

content.translation: I have three dogs

content.context: Introducing yourself

content.question: How do you say 'Excuse me' in German?

content.rule: Review: articles match gender, possessives change with gender.
content.question: _____ Katze ist schwarz. (The cat is black.)
content.explanation: 'Katze' is feminine, so it takes 'die'.

exercises.instruction: Write a short introduction in English based on this German text.

content.prompt: Read and translate the full paragraph:
content.text: Hallo! Ich heiße Maria. Ich habe eine Schwester und einen Bruder. Meine Katze ist schwarz. Ich trinke gern Kaffee. Auf Wiedersehen!
content.sampleAnswer: Hello! My name is Maria. I have a sister and a brother. My cat is black. I like to drink coffee. Goodbye!

exercises.instruction: Match all the key vocabulary from Section 1.

content.question: Complete: 'Ich _____ Anna und ich habe zwei Katzen.'

exercises.instruction: Write a short self-introduction in English from this German text.

content.text: Guten Tag! Ich heiße Thomas. Ich habe einen Bruder und eine Schwester. Mein Hund ist braun. Ich trinke gern Tee.
content.sampleAnswer: Good day! My name is Thomas. I have a brother and a sister. My dog is brown. I like to drink tea.

sections.title: People & Things
sections.description: Learn to talk about family, body parts, animals, food and drink

units.title: Extended Family
objectives: Learn vocabulary for extended family members
Practice plural forms of family nouns
Describe your extended family in simple sentences
units.grammarFocus: Plural forms of family members (die Onkel, die Tanten, die Cousins, die Cousinen)
units.culturalNote: In Germany, extended family gatherings are common on Sundays and holidays, often centered around a shared Kaffee und Kuchen (coffee and cake) tradition.

content.sentence: Meine Großmutter und mein Großvater leben in Berlin.
content.translation: My grandmother and my grandfather live in Berlin.

dialogue.speaker: app
dialogue.text: Wer ist das auf dem Foto?
dialogue.translation: Who is that in the photo?

content.translation: My grandparents live in Munich.

content.context: My aunt is named Helga.

content.question: What does 'die Großeltern' mean?

content.rule: To form the plural of feminine nouns ending in '-e', add '-n': die Tante → die Tanten.
content.question: Ich habe zwei _____.
content.explanation: 'Tante' becomes 'Tanten' in the plural. Most German feminine nouns ending in '-e' add '-n' to form the plural.

content.text: Mein Onkel und meine Tante haben drei Kinder.
content.sampleAnswer: My uncle and my aunt have three children.

units.title: Friends & People
objectives: Learn vocabulary for friends and people around you
Use indefinite articles ein, eine, ein correctly
Introduce people using simple sentences
units.grammarFocus: Indefinite articles: ein (masculine/neuter), eine (feminine) — Ein Mann, eine Frau, ein Kind
units.culturalNote: In German, 'Freund' and 'Freundin' can mean both 'friend' and 'boyfriend/girlfriend' depending on context. Adding 'mein/meine' often implies a romantic partner.

content.sentence: Das ist ein Freund und eine Freundin.
content.translation: That is a (male) friend and a (female) friend.

dialogue.speaker: app
dialogue.translation: Do you know the man over there?

content.translation: That is a child.

content.context: That is a woman. (Frau is feminine)

content.question: What does 'der Kollege' mean?

content.rule: Indefinite articles: 'ein' for masculine and neuter nouns, 'eine' for feminine nouns. Der Mann → ein Mann, die Frau → eine Frau, das Kind → ein Kind.
content.question: Das ist _____ Mann.
content.explanation: 'Mann' is masculine (der Mann), so the indefinite article in the nominative case is 'ein'.

content.sampleAnswer: My (female) friend is a teacher.

units.title: Clothing
objectives: Learn vocabulary for common clothing items
Use the verb 'tragen' (to wear) with accusative case
Describe what someone is wearing
units.grammarFocus: Accusative case with 'tragen' (to wear): Ich trage einen Mantel (masc.), eine Hose (fem.), ein Kleid (neut.)
units.culturalNote: Germans tend to dress practically for the weather. A good 'Mantel' (coat) and 'Mütze' (cap) are essentials during the cold German winters.

content.sentence: Ich trage ein Hemd und eine Hose.
content.translation: I am wearing a shirt and pants.
She is wearing a dress.

content.context: He is wearing a coat. (Mantel is masculine — accusative)

content.question: What does 'die Mütze' mean?

content.rule: After 'tragen', nouns take the accusative case. For masculine nouns: ein → einen. Feminine (eine) and neuter (ein) stay the same.
content.question: Ich trage _____ Hose.
content.explanation: 'Hose' is feminine (die Hose). In the accusative case, feminine articles do not change: eine Hose.

content.text: Sie trägt ein Kleid und Schuhe.
content.sampleAnswer: She is wearing a dress and shoes.

units.title: Home & Rooms
objectives: Learn vocabulary for rooms and types of homes
Use prepositions of place with dative case
Describe where things are in a home
units.grammarFocus: Prepositions of place with dative: in der Küche (fem.), im Schlafzimmer (neut. — im = in dem), im Haus (neut.)
units.culturalNote: Many Germans live in apartments (Wohnungen) rather than houses. Renting is very common, even for families.

content.sentence: Ich bin in der Küche.
content.translation: I am in the kitchen.
The cat is in the bedroom.

content.context: I cook in the kitchen. (Küche is feminine — dative)

content.question: What does 'das Haus' mean?

content.rule: When expressing location with 'in', use the dative case: 'in der' for feminine nouns, 'im' (in dem) for masculine and neuter nouns.
content.question: Ich bin _____ Badezimmer.
content.explanation: 'Badezimmer' is neuter (das Badezimmer). In the dative case with 'in', we use 'im' (contraction of 'in dem').

content.text: Meine Wohnung hat eine Küche und ein Wohnzimmer.
content.sampleAnswer: My apartment has a kitchen and a living room.

units.title: Furniture & Objects
objectives: Learn vocabulary for common furniture and household objects
Use 'es gibt' (there is/there are) with accusative case
Describe what is in a room
units.grammarFocus: 'Es gibt' (there is/there are) + accusative: Es gibt einen Tisch (masc.), eine Lampe (fem.), ein Bett (neut.)

content.sentence: Es gibt einen Tisch und vier Stühle.
content.translation: There is a table and four chairs.

options.text: Es gibt ein Bett und einen Schrank.

content.translation: There is a lamp.

content.context: There is a chair. (Stuhl is masculine — accusative)

content.question: What does 'das Bett' mean?

content.rule: 'Es gibt' is always followed by the accusative case. Masculine: einen, Feminine: eine, Neuter: ein.
content.question: Es gibt _____ Fenster im Zimmer.
content.explanation: 'Fenster' is neuter (das Fenster). In the accusative case, the neuter indefinite article remains 'ein'.

content.text: Es gibt einen Tisch und eine Lampe im Wohnzimmer.
content.sampleAnswer: There is a table and a lamp in the living room.

units.title: Breakfast & Meals
objectives: Learn vocabulary for meals and breakfast foods
Use 'Ich esse ... zum Frühstück' to describe meals
Talk about daily eating habits
units.grammarFocus: Meal expressions: 'Ich esse ... zum Frühstück / zum Mittagessen / zum Abendessen' (I eat ... for breakfast/lunch/dinner)
units.culturalNote: A traditional German breakfast (Frühstück) typically includes bread, cold cuts, cheese (Käse), butter (Butter), jam, and a boiled egg (Ei). Müsli is also very popular.

content.sentence: Ich esse Käse und Butter zum Frühstück.
content.translation: I eat cheese and butter for breakfast.

dialogue.speaker: app
dialogue.translation: What do you eat for breakfast?

options.text: Ich esse ein Ei und Müsli.

content.translation: I eat eggs for dinner.

content.context: I eat cereal for breakfast.

content.question: What does 'der Käse' mean?

content.rule: To say what you eat for a meal, use: 'Ich esse [food] zum [meal]'. 'zum' is a contraction of 'zu dem'.
content.question: Ich esse Käse _____ Mittagessen.
content.explanation: 'Mittagessen' is neuter (das Mittagessen). 'zu + dem' contracts to 'zum' for masculine and neuter nouns.

content.text: Zum Frühstück esse ich ein Ei mit Butter.
content.sampleAnswer: For breakfast I eat an egg with butter.

units.title: Fruits & Vegetables
objectives: Learn vocabulary for common fruits and vegetables
Practice plural forms including umlaut changes
Use fruits and vegetables in simple sentences
units.grammarFocus: Plural forms of nouns: add -n (Banane→Bananen), add -en (Kartoffel→Kartoffeln), umlaut + -e (Apfel→Äpfel)
units.culturalNote: Germany has many weekly farmers' markets (Wochenmärkte) where people buy fresh fruits and vegetables directly from local farmers.

content.sentence: Ich kaufe Äpfel und Bananen auf dem Markt.
content.translation: I buy apples and bananas at the market.
I like to eat potatoes.

content.context: I would like three apples please. (Apfel → plural with umlaut)

content.question: What is the plural of 'die Banane'?

content.rule: Some masculine nouns form their plural with an umlaut: der Apfel → die Äpfel. Feminine nouns ending in '-e' add '-n': die Tomate → die Tomaten. Nouns ending in '-el' may add '-n': die Kartoffel → die Kartoffeln.
content.question: Ich kaufe zwei _____.
content.explanation: 'Kartoffel' is feminine and forms its plural by adding '-n': die Kartoffeln. There is no umlaut change.

content.text: Ich esse einen Salat mit Tomaten und Orangen.
content.sampleAnswer: I eat a salad with tomatoes and oranges.

units.title: More Animals & Nature
objectives: Learn vocabulary for farm animals and nature
Use 'es gibt' to describe animals and nature
Talk about what you can find in nature
units.grammarFocus: 'Es gibt' + accusative with animals and nature: Es gibt eine Kuh (fem.), Es gibt ein Pferd (neut.), Es gibt einen Baum (masc.)
units.culturalNote: Germany has many beautiful natural areas. The 'Schwarzwald' (Black Forest) and the 'Bayerische Alpen' (Bavarian Alps) are famous for their wildlife and scenery.

content.sentence: Es gibt ein Pferd und eine Kuh auf dem Bauernhof.
content.translation: There is a horse and a cow on the farm.

dialogue.speaker: app
dialogue.translation: What do you see in the garden?

options.text: Es gibt Blumen und einen Schmetterling.
Es gibt einen Tisch und einen Stuhl.

content.translation: There are many trees.

content.text: Es gibt _____ Kuh auf dem Bauernhof.
content.context: There is a cow on the farm. (Kuh is feminine — accusative)

content.question: What does 'der Baum' mean?

content.rule: 'Es gibt' is followed by the accusative case. For masculine nouns: ein → einen. Feminine (eine) and neuter (ein) remain unchanged.
content.question: Es gibt _____ Schmetterling im Garten.
content.explanation: 'Schmetterling' is masculine (der Schmetterling). After 'es gibt', the masculine indefinite article changes to 'einen'.

content.text: Es gibt drei Schweine und zwei Kühe auf dem Bauernhof.
content.sampleAnswer: There are three pigs and two cows on the farm.

units.title: Health & Feelings
objectives: Learn adjectives for health and feelings
Use 'Ich bin' + adjective to describe how you feel
Ask and answer questions about well-being
units.grammarFocus: 'Ich bin' + adjective to express feelings and states: Ich bin müde (I am tired), Ich bin glücklich (I am happy)
units.culturalNote: When Germans ask 'Wie geht es dir?' (How are you?), they often expect an honest answer, not just 'fine'. It's common to say how you really feel.

content.sentence: Ich bin müde und hungrig.
content.translation: I am tired and hungry.
I am sick today.

content.text: Mein Bruder ist _____. Er braucht Wasser.
content.context: My brother is thirsty. He needs water.

content.question: What is the opposite of 'krank' (sick)?

content.rule: Use 'Ich bin' + adjective to describe your state or feelings. The adjective does not change form when used after 'sein' (to be).
content.question: Die Kinder _____ müde.
content.explanation: 'Die Kinder' (the children) is third person plural, so we use 'sind' (are). Ich bin, du bist, er/sie/es ist, wir sind, ihr seid, sie sind.

content.text: Ich bin nicht krank. Ich bin gesund und glücklich.
content.sampleAnswer: I am not sick. I am healthy and happy.

units.title: Section 2 Review
objectives: Review all vocabulary and grammar from Section 2
Practice accusative case, 'es gibt', and 'ich bin' + adjective
Demonstrate comprehension across all Section 2 topics
units.grammarFocus: Review: accusative case (einen/eine/ein), 'es gibt' + accusative, 'ich bin' + adjective, prepositions with dative

content.sentence: Ich bin glücklich. Es gibt Käse zum Frühstück.
content.translation: I am happy. There is cheese for breakfast.

content.prompt: Es gibt einen Stuhl im Wohnzimmer.

options.text: There is a chair in the living room.
There is a table in the bedroom.
There is a lamp in the kitchen.

content.translation: My (female) friend is wearing a cap.

content.context: There is a bed in the bedroom. (Bett is neuter — accusative)

content.question: Which sentence means 'I eat an egg for breakfast'?

options.text: Ich esse ein Ei zum Frühstück.
Ich esse ein Ei zum Abendessen.
Ich trage ein Ei zum Frühstück.

content.rule: Review: 'es gibt' + accusative (einen for masculine, eine for feminine, ein for neuter). 'Ich bin' + adjective for feelings. 'in der' for feminine dative, 'im' for masculine/neuter dative.
content.question: Meine Tante ist _____ Badezimmer.
content.explanation: 'Badezimmer' is neuter (das Badezimmer). For location with 'in' + dative neuter, use 'im' (contraction of 'in dem').

content.text: Mein Cousin ist müde. Er ist im Schlafzimmer.
content.sampleAnswer: My cousin is tired. He is in the bedroom.

checkpoint.title: Section 2 Checkpoint
checkpoint.description: Test your knowledge of People & Things from Section 2.

content.question: How do you say 'There is a table in the kitchen' in German?

options.text: Es gibt einen Tisch in der Küche.
Es gibt ein Tisch in der Küche.
Es gibt einer Tisch in die Küche.

content.text: Meine Großmutter trägt ein Kleid. Sie ist glücklich.
content.sampleAnswer: My grandmother is wearing a dress. She is happy.

sections.title: Daily Routines
sections.description: Talk about time, weather, places, and getting around

units.title: Telling Time
objectives: Learn vocabulary for telling time in German
Use 'Es ist ... Uhr' and 'halb/Viertel' constructions
Understand the 24-hour clock used in German-speaking countries
units.grammarFocus: 'Es ist ... Uhr' for full hours, 'Es ist halb drei' for half hours (half TO the next hour), and 'Es ist Viertel vor/nach ...' for quarter hours
units.culturalNote: Germans officially use the 24-hour clock (e.g., 14:30 instead of 2:30 PM), especially in schedules, train times, and formal settings. In casual speech, 12-hour time is also common.

content.sentence: Wie spät ist es? Es ist halb drei.
content.translation: What time is it? It is half past two.
It is half past five.

content.context: What time is it?

content.question: How do Germans say 2:30?

content.rule: In German, 'halb drei' means half TO three (2:30), not half PAST three. The 'halb' always refers to halfway to the NEXT hour.
content.question: What time is 'halb sieben'?
content.explanation: 'Halb sieben' means halfway to seven, which is 6:30. This is a common source of confusion for English speakers.

content.text: Wie spät ist es jetzt bei dir?
content.sampleAnswer: Es ist zehn Uhr.

units.title: Days of the Week
objectives: Learn the seven days of the week in German
Use 'am' with days of the week
Talk about plans on specific days
units.grammarFocus: Use 'am' (contraction of 'an dem') with days of the week: 'Am Montag gehe ich zur Schule.' All days are masculine nouns.
units.culturalNote: In Germany, the week officially starts on Monday, not Sunday. Saturdays may also be called 'Sonnabend' in northern and eastern Germany.

content.sentence: Heute ist Montag. Am Dienstag gehe ich einkaufen.
content.translation: Today is Monday. On Tuesday I go shopping.

options.text: On Friday I have a day off.

content.translation: On Thursday I work.

content.context: Today is Tuesday.

content.question: Which preposition is used with days of the week in German?

content.rule: In German, to say 'on' a day of the week, use 'am' (contraction of 'an dem'). Example: 'Am Montag gehe ich zur Schule.' (On Monday I go to school.)
content.question: How do you say 'on Wednesday' in German?
content.explanation: 'Am' is the correct preposition used with days of the week. It is a contraction of 'an dem'. 'Im' is used with months and seasons, 'um' is used with clock times.

content.sampleAnswer: Am Samstag gehe ich spazieren.

units.title: Months & Seasons
objectives: Learn the names of months and seasons in German
Use 'im' with months and seasons
Talk about events and weather in different months and seasons
units.grammarFocus: Use 'im' (contraction of 'in dem') with months and seasons: 'Im Januar schneit es.' / 'Im Sommer ist es warm.'
units.culturalNote: Germany has four distinct seasons. Christmas markets (Weihnachtsmärkte) open in late November and are a beloved tradition throughout the German-speaking world.

content.sentence: Im Frühling blühen die Blumen. Im Winter schneit es.
content.translation: In spring the flowers bloom. In winter it snows.
In summer we drive to the sea.

content.context: In autumn the leaves fall.

content.question: Which month comes after Mai (May) in German?

content.rule: In German, use 'im' (contraction of 'in dem') with months and seasons. Example: 'Im Januar schneit es.' (In January it snows.) 'Im Sommer ist es warm.' (In summer it is warm.)
content.question: How do you say 'in February' in German?
content.explanation: 'Im' is the correct contraction of 'in dem' used with months and seasons. 'Am' is for days, 'um' is for clock times.

content.text: Welche Jahreszeit magst du am liebsten und warum?
content.sampleAnswer: Ich mag den Sommer am liebsten, weil es warm ist.

units.title: Daily Schedule
objectives: Learn verbs for daily routine activities
Understand and use separable verbs in present tense
Describe a typical daily schedule
units.grammarFocus: Separable verbs: In the present tense the prefix separates and moves to the end. aufstehen → 'Ich stehe um 7 Uhr auf.'
units.culturalNote: Germans value punctuality (Pünktlichkeit) highly. Being on time for appointments and work is considered essential.

content.sentence: Ich stehe um sieben Uhr auf und frühstücke.
content.translation: I get up at seven o'clock and have breakfast.

options.text: Ich stehe um sechs Uhr auf.

content.transcript: Ich gehe um zehn Uhr nach Hause.

options.text: I go home at ten o'clock.
I go to school at ten o'clock.
I get up at ten o'clock.
I study German in the afternoon.
I learn English in the afternoon.
I study German in the morning.

content.translation: I get up at seven o'clock.

content.text: Ich _____ um acht Uhr nach Hause.
content.context: I go home at eight o'clock.

content.question: In the sentence 'Ich stehe um 7 Uhr auf', where does the separable prefix go?

options.text: At the end of the sentence

content.rule: Separable verbs split in the present tense: the prefix moves to the end of the clause. Example: aufstehen → 'Ich stehe um 7 Uhr auf.' The conjugated stem stays in second position, and the prefix goes to the end.
content.question: How do you correctly say 'I get up at 6 o'clock'?

options.text: Ich stehe um sechs Uhr auf.
Ich stehe auf um sechs Uhr.

content.explanation: The conjugated part 'stehe' stays in second position and the prefix 'auf' moves to the very end of the sentence.

content.sampleAnswer: Ich stehe um sieben Uhr auf und frühstücke.

units.title: Weather
objectives: Learn vocabulary for weather conditions
Use impersonal 'es' for weather expressions
Describe the weather in simple sentences
units.grammarFocus: Impersonal 'es' expressions for weather: 'Es regnet.' (It rains.) 'Es schneit.' (It snows.) 'Es ist warm/kalt/heiß.' (It is warm/cold/hot.)
units.culturalNote: Germans have a famous saying: 'Es gibt kein schlechtes Wetter, nur falsche Kleidung.' (There is no bad weather, only wrong clothing.) Outdoor activities are popular in all seasons.

content.sentence: Es regnet heute und es ist kalt.
content.translation: It is raining today and it is cold.
It has been raining for three days.

content.context: It is very cold today.

content.question: What does 'Es schneit' mean?

content.rule: German uses impersonal 'es' for weather. The subject is always 'es'. For verbs: 'Es regnet.' (It rains.) 'Es schneit.' (It snows.) For adjectives: 'Es ist kalt.' (It is cold.) 'Es ist warm.' (It is warm.)
content.question: How do you say 'It is warm' in German?
content.explanation: Weather expressions follow standard German word order: 'Es' (subject) + 'ist' (verb) + adjective. So 'Es ist warm.' is correct.

content.text: Wie ist das Wetter heute bei dir?
content.sampleAnswer: Es ist sonnig und warm heute.

units.title: Places in Town
objectives: Learn vocabulary for common places in town
Use 'zum' and 'zur' to express going to a place
Describe where you are going in simple sentences
units.grammarFocus: Contractions with 'zu': 'zu + dem = zum' (masculine/neuter dative), 'zu + der = zur' (feminine dative). 'Ich gehe zum Supermarkt.' / 'Ich gehe zur Post.'
units.culturalNote: Apotheken (pharmacies) in Germany display a distinctive red 'A' sign and are separate from Drogerien (drugstores). Prescription medicine is only available at the Apotheke.

content.sentence: Ich gehe zum Supermarkt und dann zur Post.
content.translation: I go to the supermarket and then to the post office.

content.transcript: Das Krankenhaus ist neben dem Bahnhof.

options.text: The hospital is next to the train station.
The bank is next to the train station.
The hospital is next to the school.
I go to the post office.

content.translation: I go to the train station.

content.context: I go to school. (die Schule = feminine)

content.question: Which is the correct contraction of 'zu + der' (feminine)?

content.rule: 'Zu' (to) contracts with dative articles: 'zu + dem = zum' (masculine/neuter), 'zu + der = zur' (feminine). Example: 'Ich gehe zum Supermarkt.' (masculine) 'Ich gehe zur Post.' (feminine)
content.question: How do you say 'I go to the hospital'? (das Krankenhaus = neuter)
content.explanation: 'Krankenhaus' is neuter (das Krankenhaus), so in the dative case after 'zu' we use 'zum' (zu + dem). 'Zur' would be for feminine nouns.

content.sampleAnswer: Am Samstag gehe ich zum Supermarkt.

units.title: Directions
objectives: Learn vocabulary for giving and understanding directions
Use the formal imperative for giving directions
Ask for and give directions to places in town
units.grammarFocus: Formal imperative for directions: Verb + 'Sie'. 'Gehen Sie geradeaus!' (Go straight ahead!) 'Biegen Sie links ab!' (Turn left!)
units.culturalNote: Germans generally give directions using landmarks and street names. It is polite to start with 'Entschuldigung' (excuse me) when asking for directions.

content.sentence: Gehen Sie geradeaus und dann links an der Ampel.
content.translation: Go straight ahead and then left at the traffic light.

dialogue.speaker: app
dialogue.translation: Excuse me, where is the train station?

options.text: Gehen Sie geradeaus und dann rechts.

content.transcript: Biegen Sie links an der Kreuzung ab.

content.prompt: Die Apotheke ist gegenüber der Post.

options.text: The pharmacy is across from the post office.
The pharmacy is next to the post office.
The pharmacy is behind the post office.

content.translation: Go straight ahead to the traffic light.

content.context: The bank is next to the supermarket.

content.question: What does 'geradeaus' mean?

content.rule: The formal imperative uses the verb first, followed by 'Sie': 'Gehen Sie geradeaus!' (Go straight ahead!) 'Biegen Sie rechts ab!' (Turn right!) This is the polite way to give directions in German.
content.question: How do you politely say 'Turn left!' in German?
content.explanation: In the formal imperative the verb comes first, then 'Sie'. The separable prefix 'ab' goes to the end: 'Biegen Sie links ab!'

content.text: Erklären Sie den Weg zum Supermarkt.
content.sampleAnswer: Gehen Sie geradeaus und dann rechts an der Ampel.

units.title: Transportation
objectives: Learn vocabulary for common means of transportation
Use 'mit + dative' to express traveling by a certain vehicle
Talk about how you get to places
units.grammarFocus: 'Mit' (with/by) always takes the dative case: der → dem, die → der, das → dem. 'Ich fahre mit dem Bus.' / 'Ich fahre mit der U-Bahn.' / 'Ich fahre mit dem Auto.'
units.culturalNote: Germany has an excellent public transportation network. Deutsche Bahn operates the national railway, and most cities have buses, trams, and U-Bahn (subway) systems.

content.sentence: Ich fahre mit dem Zug zur Arbeit.
content.translation: I travel by train to work.

dialogue.speaker: app
dialogue.translation: How do you get to school?

content.transcript: Wir fliegen im Sommer nach Spanien.

options.text: We fly to Spain in summer.
We drive to Spain in summer.
We fly to Spain in winter.

content.translation: We travel by subway.

content.text: Ich fahre _____ dem Auto zur Arbeit.
content.context: I drive by car to work.

content.question: Which dative article follows 'mit' for 'der Bus' (masculine)?

content.rule: 'Mit' (with/by) always requires the dative case. Articles change: der → dem (masculine), die → der (feminine), das → dem (neuter). Examples: 'mit dem Bus', 'mit der U-Bahn', 'mit dem Auto'.
content.question: How do you say 'by subway' (die U-Bahn) correctly?
content.explanation: 'Die U-Bahn' is feminine. In the dative case after 'mit', 'die' becomes 'der'. So it is 'mit der U-Bahn'.

content.text: Wie fährst du zur Arbeit oder zur Schule?
content.sampleAnswer: Ich fahre mit dem Bus zur Schule.

units.title: Shopping Basics
objectives: Learn vocabulary for basic shopping situations
Ask about prices using 'Wie viel kostet ...?'
Use numbers to talk about prices in euros
units.grammarFocus: 'Wie viel kostet ...?' (How much does ... cost?) for singular, 'Wie viel kosten ...?' for plural. Numbers + Euro for prices: 'Das kostet fünf Euro.'
units.culturalNote: Cash (Bargeld) is still widely used in Germany. Many smaller shops and restaurants do not accept credit cards, so it is wise to carry some cash.

content.sentence: Wie viel kostet das? Das kostet fünf Euro.
content.translation: How much does that cost? That costs five euros.

dialogue.speaker: app
dialogue.translation: How much does the book cost?

options.text: I would like to pay with cash.
I would like to buy with cash.
I would like to pay with card.

content.translation: How much does the bread cost?

content.context: The T-shirt costs twenty euros.

content.question: What does 'billig' mean?

content.rule: Use 'Wie viel kostet ...?' for singular items and 'Wie viel kosten ...?' for plural items. The verb must agree in number with the subject. 'Wie viel kostet der Kaffee?' / 'Wie viel kosten die Äpfel?'
content.question: How do you ask 'How much do the shoes cost?'
content.explanation: 'Die Schuhe' (the shoes) is plural, so the verb must be 'kosten' (plural conjugation), not 'kostet' (singular).

content.text: Du bist im Supermarkt. Was kaufst du?
content.sampleAnswer: Ich kaufe Brot und Milch.

units.title: Section 3 Review
objectives: Review all vocabulary and grammar from Section 3
Practice separable verbs, weather expressions, mit + dative, and directions
Combine daily routine topics in realistic contexts
units.grammarFocus: Review: separable verbs (aufstehen), impersonal 'es' for weather, 'mit + dative' for transportation, 'zum/zur' for places, formal imperative for directions, 'am' with days, 'im' with months/seasons

content.sentence: Am Montag stehe ich um sieben Uhr auf und fahre mit dem Bus zur Arbeit.
content.translation: On Monday I get up at seven o'clock and travel by bus to work.

dialogue.speaker: app
dialogue.text: Wie ist das Wetter und was machst du heute?
dialogue.translation: How is the weather and what are you doing today?

options.text: Es ist sonnig. Ich gehe zum Supermarkt.

content.transcript: Im Winter fahre ich mit dem Zug zur Arbeit.

options.text: In winter I travel by train to work.
In summer I travel by train to work.
In winter I travel by bus to work.

content.prompt: Gehen Sie links an der Ampel.

options.text: Go left at the traffic light.
Go right at the traffic light.
Go straight at the traffic light.

content.translation: It is half past two and it is raining.

content.text: Ich fahre _____ dem Fahrrad zur Schule.
content.context: I ride by bicycle to school.

content.question: What does 'Ich stehe um sechs Uhr auf' mean?

options.text: I get up at six o'clock.
I go to sleep at six o'clock.
I stand up at six o'clock.

content.rule: Review: 'am' with days (Am Montag), 'im' with months/seasons (Im Januar, Im Sommer), 'mit + dative' for transport (mit dem Bus), 'zum/zur' for places. Separable prefixes go to the end.
content.question: Which sentence is grammatically correct?

options.text: Am Freitag fahre ich mit dem Zug nach Berlin.
Im Freitag fahre ich mit dem Zug nach Berlin.
Am Freitag fahre ich mit den Zug nach Berlin.

content.explanation: Days use 'am' (not 'im'). 'Mit' requires the dative case: 'dem Zug' (not 'den Zug', which is accusative).

content.sampleAnswer: Ich stehe um sieben Uhr auf und fahre mit dem Bus zur Arbeit.

checkpoint.title: Section 3 Checkpoint
checkpoint.description: Test your knowledge of daily routines, time, weather, places, directions, transportation, and shopping.

content.question: How do you say 'I travel by train' in German?

content.text: Beschreibe deinen Tag: Wann stehst du auf? Wie fährst du zur Arbeit? Wie ist das Wetter?
content.sampleAnswer: Ich stehe um sieben Uhr auf. Ich fahre mit dem Bus zur Arbeit. Es ist warm und sonnig.

sections.title: Social Situations
sections.description: Handle ordering at a cafe, shopping, asking directions, and dining out

units.title: At the Café
objectives: Learn vocabulary for ordering at a café
Use polite ordering phrases: 'Ich möchte ...' and 'Ich hätte gern ...'
Understand tipping culture in Germany
units.grammarFocus: 'Ich möchte ...' (I would like ...) and 'Ich hätte gern ...' (I'd gladly have ...) are polite ways to order in German cafés and restaurants.
units.culturalNote: In Germany, tipping is customary but more modest than in the US. It is common to round up the bill or add 5–10%. You tell the waiter the total you want to pay, including the tip.

content.sentence: Ich möchte eine Tasse Kaffee, bitte.
content.translation: I would like a cup of coffee, please.

dialogue.speaker: app
dialogue.translation: What would you like to drink?

content.translation: I would like to order, please.

content.context: I would gladly have a cup of tea.

content.question: What does 'der Kellner' mean?

content.rule: 'Ich möchte ...' and 'Ich hätte gern ...' are polite ordering forms. 'Ich will ...' (I want) sounds demanding and is considered impolite when ordering.
content.question: Which is the most polite way to order a coffee?
content.explanation: 'Ich hätte gern ...' and 'Ich möchte ...' are the standard polite forms for ordering. 'Ich will ...' sounds demanding, and 'Gib mir ...' (Give me ...) is rude.

content.sampleAnswer: Ich möchte eine Tasse Kaffee, bitte.

units.title: At the Restaurant
objectives: Learn vocabulary for dining at a restaurant
Use modal verbs 'können' and 'dürfen' to make polite requests
Order a meal using correct German phrases
units.grammarFocus: Modal verbs for polite requests: 'Kann ich ... haben?' (Can I have ...?) and 'Darf ich ...?' (May I ...?). The main verb goes to the end in infinitive form.
units.culturalNote: In German restaurants, you often seat yourself. Water is not free — you must order 'Mineralwasser' (mineral water). Saying 'Zahlen, bitte!' (Check, please!) signals the waiter you are ready to pay.

content.sentence: Kann ich bitte die Speisekarte haben?
content.translation: Can I please have the menu?

options.text: Ja, ich hätte gern das Hauptgericht.

content.transcript: Kann ich bitte das Dessert haben?

options.text: Can I please have the dessert?
Can I please have the starter?
Can I please have the bill?

content.translation: Can I have the menu?

content.text: _____ ich bitte ein Glas Wasser haben?
content.context: Can I please have a glass of water?

content.question: What does 'die Vorspeise' mean?

content.rule: With modal verbs like 'können' (can) and 'dürfen' (may), the modal verb is conjugated in second position and the main verb goes to the end in infinitive form: 'Kann ich die Speisekarte haben?'
content.question: How do you correctly say 'May I order?'
content.explanation: In a question with a modal verb, the conjugated modal 'darf' comes first, followed by the subject 'ich', and the infinitive 'bestellen' goes to the end.

content.prompt: Ask politely for the main course:
content.sampleAnswer: Ich hätte gern das Hauptgericht, bitte.

units.title: Shopping for Clothes
objectives: Learn vocabulary for clothing shopping
Use 'zu' + adjective to express something is too big, small, or expensive
Ask to try on clothes and describe fit
units.grammarFocus: 'Zu' + adjective means 'too' + adjective: zu groß (too big), zu klein (too small), zu teuer (too expensive), zu eng (too tight).
units.culturalNote: In German shops, clothing sizes follow European standards. It is polite to ask 'Darf ich das anprobieren?' (May I try this on?) before using the fitting room.

content.sentence: Darf ich das anprobieren? Das ist zu groß.
content.translation: May I try this on? It is too big.
The pants are too big.

content.text: Das Hemd ist zu _____. Haben Sie Größe M?
content.context: The shirt is too small. Do you have size M?

content.question: What does 'anprobieren' mean?

content.rule: 'Zu' placed before an adjective means 'too': zu groß (too big), zu klein (too small), zu teuer (too expensive), zu lang (too long). It always expresses excess.
content.question: How do you say 'The shoes are too small' in German?
content.explanation: 'Zu' comes directly before the adjective: 'zu klein'. 'Sehr klein' means 'very small', which is different. The word order 'klein zu' is incorrect in German.

content.prompt: Tell the shop assistant the shirt does not fit:
content.sampleAnswer: Nein, das Hemd ist zu groß.

units.title: At the Market
objectives: Learn vocabulary for shopping at an outdoor market
Use quantity expressions: 'ein Kilo ...', 'zwei Stück ...'
Ask for prices and quantities in German
units.grammarFocus: Quantity expressions: 'Ein Kilo Äpfel' (one kilo of apples), 'Zwei Stück Kuchen' (two pieces of cake). No extra preposition is needed between the quantity and the noun.
units.culturalNote: Weekly outdoor markets (Wochenmärkte) are very popular in Germany. You can buy fresh fruit, vegetables, bread, cheese, and flowers. Many Germans prefer shopping at markets for fresh produce.

content.sentence: Ich hätte gern ein Kilo Äpfel, bitte.
content.translation: I would like one kilogram of apples, please.
Two pieces of cake, please.

content.context: I would like one kilogram of potatoes.

content.question: What does 'günstig' mean?

content.rule: In German, quantity expressions do not use a preposition: 'ein Kilo Äpfel' (one kilo of apples), 'zwei Stück Kuchen' (two pieces of cake). Unlike English, there is no 'of' between the quantity and the noun.
content.question: How do you say 'three kilos of oranges'?
content.explanation: No preposition is needed: 'Drei Kilo Orangen'. Also note that 'Kilo' does not change in the plural in German — it stays 'Kilo', not 'Kilos'.

content.prompt: Ask for something at the market:
content.sampleAnswer: Ich hätte gern zwei Kilo Tomaten, bitte.

units.title: Asking for Directions
objectives: Learn vocabulary for asking and giving directions
Use 'Wo ist ...?' and 'Wie komme ich zum/zur ...?' questions
Understand basic location prepositions and direction words
units.grammarFocus: 'Wo ist ...?' (Where is ...?) for asking locations. 'Wie komme ich zum ...?' (masc./neut.) and 'Wie komme ich zur ...?' (fem.) for asking how to get somewhere. 'Zum' = zu dem, 'zur' = zu der.
units.culturalNote: Germans are generally happy to help with directions. In cities, most streets have clear signs. Learning 'links' (left), 'rechts' (right), and 'geradeaus' (straight ahead) will help you navigate easily.

content.sentence: Entschuldigung, wo ist der Bahnhof?
content.translation: Excuse me, where is the train station?

dialogue.speaker: app
dialogue.translation: How do I get to the bridge?

options.text: Gehen Sie geradeaus und dann links.

content.translation: How do I get to the market?

content.context: How do I get to the bridge? (Brücke is feminine)

content.question: What does 'nah' mean?

content.rule: 'Wie komme ich zum ...?' is used with masculine and neuter nouns (zum = zu dem). 'Wie komme ich zur ...?' is used with feminine nouns (zur = zu der).
content.question: How do you ask 'How do I get to the street?' (die Straße — feminine)
content.explanation: 'Straße' is feminine (die Straße), so you use 'zur' (zu der). 'Zum' (zu dem) is only for masculine and neuter nouns.

content.prompt: Ask for directions to the train station:
content.sampleAnswer: Wo ist der Bahnhof, bitte?

units.title: Public Transportation
objectives: Learn vocabulary for using public transportation
Ask about schedules using 'Wann fährt ...?'
Understand time expressions related to transportation
units.grammarFocus: 'Wann fährt ...?' (When does ... leave?) for asking about departure times. Time expressions: 'um 10 Uhr' (at 10 o'clock), 'in fünf Minuten' (in five minutes), 'jede halbe Stunde' (every half hour).
units.culturalNote: Germany has an excellent public transportation system. Deutsche Bahn runs trains nationwide. Cities have U-Bahn (subway), S-Bahn (commuter rail), Straßenbahn (tram), and Bus networks. Tickets must be validated before boarding.

content.sentence: Wann fährt der nächste Bus zum Bahnhof?
content.translation: When does the next bus to the train station leave?

dialogue.speaker: app
dialogue.text: Wo kann ich eine Fahrkarte kaufen?
dialogue.translation: Where can I buy a ticket?

content.transcript: Sie müssen an der nächsten Haltestelle umsteigen.

options.text: You must transfer at the next stop.
You must get off at the next stop.
You must buy a ticket at the next stop.

content.translation: When does the train leave?

content.context: The bus leaves in ten minutes.

content.question: What does 'der Fahrplan' mean?

content.rule: 'Wann fährt ...?' (When does ... leave?) is the standard question for departure times. Answer with 'um' + time: 'Der Zug fährt um 14 Uhr.' (The train leaves at 2 PM.) or 'in' + duration: 'in fünf Minuten' (in five minutes).
content.question: How do you say 'The bus leaves at 3 o'clock'?

options.text: Der Bus fährt um drei Uhr.
Der Bus fährt bei drei Uhr.
Der Bus fährt an drei Uhr.

content.explanation: Use 'um' for clock times: 'um drei Uhr' (at three o'clock). 'Bei' and 'an' are not used with clock times in this way.

content.prompt: Ask when the next train leaves:
content.sampleAnswer: Wann fährt der nächste Zug?

units.title: At the Hotel
objectives: Learn vocabulary for staying at a hotel
Use separable verbs 'einchecken' and 'auschecken'
Make a reservation and ask about room types
units.grammarFocus: Separable verbs: 'einchecken' → 'Ich checke ein' and 'auschecken' → 'Ich checke aus'. In present tense, the prefix separates and moves to the end of the clause.
units.culturalNote: In German hotels, breakfast (Frühstück) is often included in the room price. Check-in is usually from 14:00 or 15:00, and check-out by 10:00 or 11:00. It is common to present your passport at check-in.

content.sentence: Ich möchte einchecken. Ich habe eine Reservierung.
content.translation: I would like to check in. I have a reservation.

dialogue.speaker: app
dialogue.text: Möchten Sie ein Einzelzimmer oder ein Doppelzimmer?
dialogue.translation: Would you like a single room or a double room?

content.translation: I check out at ten o'clock.

content.context: I check in tomorrow morning. (einchecken — separable verb)

content.question: What does 'das Einzelzimmer' mean?

content.rule: Separable verbs like 'einchecken' and 'auschecken' split in the present tense. The prefix ('ein' / 'aus') moves to the end: 'Ich checke ein.' 'Wir checken um 11 Uhr aus.'
content.question: How do you correctly say 'I check out at 11 o'clock'?

options.text: Ich checke um elf Uhr aus.
Ich checke aus um elf Uhr.

content.explanation: The conjugated part 'checke' stays in second position, and the prefix 'aus' goes to the very end of the sentence, after all other elements.

content.prompt: Tell the receptionist you want to check in:
content.text: Guten Tag, wie kann ich Ihnen helfen?
content.sampleAnswer: Ich möchte einchecken. Ich habe eine Reservierung.

units.title: Making Phone Calls
objectives: Learn vocabulary for making and receiving phone calls
Use telephone phrases and the separable verb 'anrufen'
Leave a simple message and identify yourself on the phone
units.grammarFocus: The separable verb 'anrufen' splits in present tense: 'Ich rufe dich an.' (I call you.) 'auflegen' also splits: 'Bitte legen Sie nicht auf!' (Please don't hang up!)
units.culturalNote: When answering the phone in Germany, it is customary to state your last name: 'Müller?' or 'Hier ist Müller.' rather than just saying 'Hallo'. In formal contexts, say 'Guten Tag, hier spricht ...' (Good day, this is ... speaking).

content.sentence: Ich rufe dich morgen an. Was ist deine Nummer?
content.translation: I will call you tomorrow. What is your number?

options.text: Hier spricht Anna. Kann ich eine Nachricht hinterlassen?

content.translation: I will call you tomorrow.

content.context: Can I leave a message?

content.question: In Germany, how do people typically answer the phone?

options.text: By staying silent until the caller speaks

content.rule: 'Anrufen' is a separable verb: 'Ich rufe dich an.' (I call you.) The prefix 'an' separates and moves to the end. 'Auflegen' works the same way: 'Er legt auf.' (He hangs up.)
content.question: How do you correctly say 'She calls her mother'?
content.explanation: The conjugated 'ruft' stays in second position. The object 'ihre Mutter' comes next, and the prefix 'an' goes to the very end of the sentence.

content.sampleAnswer: Hier spricht Thomas. Ich rufe wegen morgen an.

units.title: Emergencies & Help
objectives: Learn vocabulary for emergencies and asking for help
Use the imperative to give urgent commands: 'Rufen Sie ... an!', 'Helfen Sie mir!'
Communicate basic emergency information
units.grammarFocus: The formal imperative (Sie-form): 'Rufen Sie die Polizei an!' (Call the police!), 'Helfen Sie mir!' (Help me!). The verb comes first, followed by 'Sie', then the rest of the sentence.
units.culturalNote: In Germany, the emergency number is 112 (fire and ambulance) and 110 (police). 112 works across all EU countries. When calling, state your name, location, what happened, and how many people are injured.

content.sentence: Hilfe! Rufen Sie bitte den Krankenwagen!
content.translation: Help! Please call the ambulance!

options.text: Es ist ein Notfall! Rufen Sie einen Arzt!

content.translation: Call the police!

content.context: Help me, please! (formal imperative)

content.question: What is the emergency number for ambulance and fire in Germany?

content.rule: The formal imperative (Sie-form) puts the verb first, followed by 'Sie': 'Rufen Sie einen Arzt!' (Call a doctor!), 'Helfen Sie mir!' (Help me!). For separable verbs: 'Rufen Sie die Polizei an!' — the prefix goes to the end.
content.question: How do you say 'Call an ambulance!' (formal)?
content.explanation: In the formal imperative, the verb 'rufen' comes first, followed by 'Sie', then the object. 'Sie rufen ...' would be a statement, not a command.

content.prompt: Ask someone to help you in an emergency:
content.text: Sie sehen einen Notfall. Was sagen Sie?
content.sampleAnswer: Hilfe! Rufen Sie bitte den Krankenwagen!

units.title: Section 4 Review
objectives: Review polite ordering and request phrases from Section 4
Practice question forms: Wo ist ...?, Wann fährt ...?, Kann ich ...?
Reinforce imperatives, separable verbs, and 'zu' + adjective
units.grammarFocus: Review of key grammar from Section 4: polite requests (Ich möchte / Ich hätte gern), modal verbs (Kann ich ...?), 'zu' + adjective, quantity expressions, Wo/Wie questions, separable verbs, and the formal imperative.
units.culturalNote: Knowing polite phrases and emergency vocabulary is essential for any traveler in Germany. Germans appreciate when visitors make an effort to speak German, even at a beginner level. A simple 'Entschuldigung' and 'Danke' go a long way.

content.sentence: Entschuldigung, ich hätte gern die Rechnung. Und wo ist die Haltestelle?
content.translation: Excuse me, I would like the bill. And where is the bus stop?

dialogue.speaker: app
dialogue.text: Guten Tag, was kann ich für Sie tun?
dialogue.translation: Good day, what can I do for you?

options.text: Ich möchte ein Einzelzimmer für zwei Nächte.

content.prompt: Ich checke um elf Uhr aus.

options.text: I check out at eleven o'clock.
I check in at eleven o'clock.

content.translation: Call a doctor!

content.context: How do I get to the train station? (Bahnhof is masculine)

content.question: How do you politely ask for the menu in German?

options.text: Kann ich bitte die Speisekarte haben?

content.rule: Review: 'zum' (zu dem) is used with masculine/neuter nouns, 'zur' (zu der) with feminine nouns. 'Ich möchte ...' / 'Ich hätte gern ...' for polite requests. Separable verbs split in present tense. Formal imperative: Verb + Sie.
content.question: Which sentence is grammatically correct?
content.explanation: 'Anrufen' is a separable verb. In the present tense, 'rufe' stays in second position and the prefix 'an' goes to the very end of the clause.

content.prompt: You are at a café. Order a drink and ask for the bill:
content.sampleAnswer: Ich hätte gern eine Tasse Tee. Die Rechnung, bitte.

checkpoint.title: Section 4 Checkpoint – Social Situations
checkpoint.description: Test your knowledge of café and restaurant vocabulary, directions, transportation, hotel check-in, phone calls, and emergencies from Section 4.

content.question: How do you politely say 'I would like a double room' in German?

content.prompt: You need help in an emergency. Ask someone to call the police and tell them it is an emergency:
content.sampleAnswer: Es ist ein Notfall! Rufen Sie bitte die Polizei!

sections.title: Building Sentences
sections.description: Learn present tense verbs, adjectives, describing people, and review everything

units.title: Regular Verbs (Present Tense)
objectives: Learn common regular verbs: machen, spielen, lernen, wohnen, kaufen, arbeiten
Conjugate regular verbs in the present tense for all persons
Build simple sentences using regular verbs
units.grammarFocus: Regular verbs in the present tense follow a predictable pattern: remove -en from the infinitive and add personal endings: ich -e, du -st, er/sie/es -t, wir -en, ihr -t, sie/Sie -en. Example: machen → ich mache, du machst, er macht, wir machen, ihr macht, sie machen.
units.culturalNote: Germans value a strong work ethic. The saying 'Arbeit macht das Leben süß' (Work makes life sweet) reflects this attitude. However, work-life balance is also prized — many shops close early on Saturdays and remain closed on Sundays.

content.sentence: Ich lerne Deutsch und er spielt Fußball.
content.translation: I learn German and he plays soccer.

exercises.instruction: Match the verbs to their meanings.
Arrange the words to form a sentence.

content.translation: We live in Munich.

content.context: He plays soccer in the park.

content.question: How do you say 'she buys' in German?

content.rule: Regular verbs: remove -en and add endings (ich -e, du -st, er -t, wir -en, ihr -t, sie -en). Verbs whose stem ends in -t or -d (like 'arbeiten') add an extra -e before -st and -t for pronunciation: du arbeitest, er arbeitet.
content.question: Which is the correct conjugation of 'arbeiten' for 'du'?
content.explanation: Verbs whose stem ends in -t or -d add an extra -e before the -st and -t endings for ease of pronunciation: du arbeitest, er arbeitet, ihr arbeitet.

content.prompt: Say what you do every day:
content.sampleAnswer: Ich lerne Deutsch und arbeite im Büro.

units.title: Irregular Verbs: sein & haben
objectives: Learn the irregular verbs sein (to be), haben (to have), and werden (to become)
Conjugate sein and haben in the present tense for all persons
Describe yourself and others using sein and haben
units.grammarFocus: sein and haben are irregular and must be memorized. sein: ich bin, du bist, er/sie/es ist, wir sind, ihr seid, sie/Sie sind. haben: ich habe, du hast, er/sie/es hat, wir haben, ihr habt, sie/Sie haben. These are the two most important German verbs.
units.culturalNote: In German, you say 'Ich bin 25 Jahre alt' (I am 25 years old) using 'sein', but 'Ich habe Hunger' (I have hunger) using 'haben'. Many states that English expresses with 'to be' use 'haben' in German: Hunger haben, Durst haben, Angst haben.

content.sentence: Ich bin müde und er hat Hunger.
content.translation: I am tired and he is hungry.

exercises.instruction: Arrange the words to form a sentence.

content.translation: I have two brothers.

content.context: I am 25 years old.

content.question: What is the correct form: 'er _____ krank'?

content.rule: In German, some conditions use 'haben' where English uses 'to be': Hunger haben (to be hungry), Durst haben (to be thirsty), Angst haben (to be afraid). But physical/emotional states often use 'sein': müde sein (to be tired), krank sein (to be sick).
content.question: How do you say 'I am hungry' in German?
content.explanation: German uses 'haben' (to have) for hunger and thirst: 'Ich habe Hunger' (literally: I have hunger). You can also say 'Ich bin hungrig' (I am hungry), but 'Ich habe Hunger' is more common in everyday speech.

content.prompt: Describe yourself using sein and haben:
content.sampleAnswer: Ich bin 20 Jahre alt und habe eine Schwester.

units.title: Separable Verbs
objectives: Learn common separable verbs: aufstehen, einkaufen, anfangen, aufmachen, zumachen, mitkommen
Understand that the prefix separates and moves to the end of the clause
Describe daily routines using separable verbs

newVocabulary.definition: to get up / to stand up

units.grammarFocus: Separable verbs have a prefix (auf-, ein-, an-, zu-, mit-) that detaches and moves to the end of the sentence in the present tense. Example: aufstehen → 'Ich stehe um 7 Uhr auf.' The prefix 'auf' goes to the end. einkaufen → 'Ich kaufe im Supermarkt ein.'
units.culturalNote: Germans tend to be early risers. Many bakeries open at 6 a.m. and offices often start at 8 a.m. The phrase 'Der frühe Vogel fängt den Wurm' (The early bird catches the worm) exists in German too!

content.sentence: Ich stehe um sieben Uhr auf.
content.translation: I get up at seven o'clock.

dialogue.speaker: app
dialogue.translation: Are you coming along to the cinema?

exercises.instruction: Arrange the words to form a sentence.

content.translation: I open the door.

content.context: When does the movie start?

content.question: What does 'aufstehen' mean?

content.rule: In separable verbs, the prefix moves to the end of the main clause in the present tense. The main verb is conjugated normally in second position: 'Ich stehe um 7 Uhr auf.' 'Er kauft im Supermarkt ein.'
content.question: How do you correctly say 'She gets up early'?
content.explanation: The prefix 'auf' separates from 'stehen' and goes to the end: 'Sie steht früh auf.' The conjugated part 'steht' stays in second position, and the prefix 'auf' moves to the end.

content.sampleAnswer: Ich stehe um acht Uhr auf.

units.title: Adjectives & Descriptions
objectives: Learn common adjectives: groß, klein, alt, jung, neu, schön, schnell, langsam
Understand predicative adjectives (no ending) vs attributive adjectives (with ending)
Describe people, places, and things using adjectives
units.grammarFocus: Predicative adjectives (after 'sein') have no ending: 'Das Haus ist groß.' Attributive adjectives (before a noun) take an ending that matches gender, number, and case: 'das große Haus' (neuter), 'der große Mann' (masculine), 'die große Frau' (feminine).
units.culturalNote: Germans often describe things as 'schön' (beautiful/nice) — from weather ('schönes Wetter') to experiences ('ein schöner Tag'). The phrase 'Na schön' can also mean 'alright then' in casual conversation.

content.sentence: Das Haus ist groß und der Garten ist schön.
content.translation: The house is big and the garden is beautiful.

exercises.instruction: Arrange the words to form a sentence.

content.translation: The new car is fast.

content.context: The train is very fast.

content.question: What does 'langsam' mean?

content.rule: Predicative adjectives (after 'sein') take NO ending: 'Das Haus ist groß.' Attributive adjectives (before a noun) MUST take an ending: 'das große Haus'. The ending depends on gender, case, and the article used.
content.question: Which sentence correctly uses the attributive adjective?
content.explanation: Before a neuter noun with 'ein', the adjective takes the ending -es: 'ein altes Haus'. Without the ending ('ein alt Haus') is grammatically incorrect. The ending -en ('ein alten Haus') would be used in the masculine accusative or dative, not here.

content.sampleAnswer: Meine Stadt ist groß und sehr schön.

units.title: Comparisons & Opinions
objectives: Learn comparative forms: besser, größer, mehr, lieber, am liebsten
Use 'als' to make comparisons between two things
Express preferences and opinions using 'gern', 'lieber', 'am liebsten', and 'ich finde'
units.grammarFocus: Comparatives are formed by adding -er to the adjective, often with an umlaut on a/o/u: groß → größer, alt → älter, gut → besser (irregular). Use 'als' for 'than': 'Berlin ist größer als München.' Preferences: gern → lieber → am liebsten: 'Ich trinke gern Tee, aber lieber Kaffee, und am liebsten Wasser.'
units.culturalNote: Germans are known for being direct when expressing opinions. Phrases like 'Ich finde das gut' (I think that's good) or 'Ich finde das schlecht' (I think that's bad) are perfectly acceptable in everyday conversation and not considered rude.

content.sentence: Berlin ist größer als München.
content.translation: Berlin is bigger than Munich.

dialogue.speaker: app
dialogue.text: Was isst du lieber, Pizza oder Pasta?
dialogue.translation: What do you prefer to eat, pizza or pasta?

options.text: I like drinking coffee most of all.

exercises.instruction: Arrange the words to form a sentence.

content.translation: I like eating pizza most of all.

content.context: Berlin is bigger than Munich.

content.question: What does 'gern' mean?

content.rule: Comparatives add -er to the adjective. Many common adjectives also get an umlaut: groß → größer, alt → älter, jung → jünger. Some are irregular: gut → besser, viel → mehr, gern → lieber. Always use 'als' for 'than'.
content.question: What is the comparative of 'alt' (old)?
content.explanation: 'Alt' gets an umlaut in the comparative: 'älter' (older). This umlaut pattern is common with one-syllable adjectives that have a, o, or u: groß → größer, jung → jünger, kurz → kürzer.

content.sampleAnswer: Ich finde Sommer besser als Winter.

units.title: Question Words
objectives: Learn all essential W-question words: wer, was, wo, wann, warum, wie, wie viel, welcher
Form questions with the verb in second position
Ask questions about people, places, time, and reasons
units.grammarFocus: W-questions place the question word first and the conjugated verb second: 'Wann kommst du?' (When are you coming?), 'Wo wohnst du?' (Where do you live?). The subject comes after the verb. 'Welcher' changes with gender: welcher (m), welche (f/pl), welches (n).
units.culturalNote: Germans appreciate directness. Asking direct questions like 'Warum?' (Why?) or 'Wie viel kostet das?' (How much does that cost?) is perfectly normal and expected. It is not considered rude to be straightforward.

content.sentence: Wann kommst du nach Berlin?
content.translation: When are you coming to Berlin?

exercises.instruction: Arrange the words to form a question.

content.translation: How much does that cost?

content.context: Where do you live?

content.question: What does 'wie viel' mean?

content.rule: In W-questions, the question word is in first position and the conjugated verb is always in second position: 'Wann kommst du?' The subject follows the verb. This verb-second rule is fundamental to German sentence structure.
content.question: Which question has the correct word order?
content.explanation: The question word 'wo' takes first position, the verb 'wohnst' must come second, and the subject 'du' follows: 'Wo wohnst du?' Putting the subject before the verb ('Wo du wohnst?') is incorrect for a direct question.

content.prompt: Ask a new friend two questions:
content.text: Du triffst jemanden zum ersten Mal.
content.sampleAnswer: Wo wohnst du? Was machst du gern?

units.title: Negation
objectives: Learn negation words: nicht, kein, nie, nichts, niemand
Understand the difference between 'nicht' and 'kein'
Negate verbs, adjectives, and nouns correctly
units.grammarFocus: Use 'nicht' to negate verbs, adjectives, and adverbs: 'Ich spiele nicht.' (I don't play.) Use 'kein' to negate nouns that would have 'ein' or no article: 'Ich habe keinen Hund.' (I don't have a dog.) 'Kein' takes the same endings as 'ein': kein (m/n nom), keine (f/pl), keinen (m acc).
units.culturalNote: Germans can seem very direct when saying 'Nein' (No). Unlike in some cultures, a straightforward 'Nein, danke' (No, thank you) is perfectly polite and common. There is no need to soften the refusal excessively.

content.sentence: Ich habe keinen Hund. Ich habe eine Katze.
content.translation: I don't have a dog. I have a cat.

exercises.instruction: Arrange the words to form a sentence.

content.translation: I don't have a brother.

content.context: That is not my book.

content.question: How do you say 'I have no time'?

content.rule: Use 'kein' to negate nouns with 'ein' or no article: 'Ich habe einen Hund' → 'Ich habe keinen Hund.' Use 'nicht' for everything else — verbs, adjectives, adverbs, and nouns with definite articles: 'Ich spiele nicht.' 'Das ist nicht der Lehrer.'
content.question: Which sentence correctly negates 'Ich trinke Kaffee' (I drink coffee)?
content.explanation: 'Kaffee' is a masculine noun used without an article, so we negate with 'kein'. Since 'Kaffee' is masculine and in the accusative case (direct object), 'kein' takes the -en ending: 'keinen Kaffee'. 'Nicht Kaffee' is incorrect, and 'kein Kaffee' has the wrong ending.

content.prompt: Say what you don't have or don't do:
content.sampleAnswer: Ich habe kein Auto und keine Katze.

units.title: Prepositions
objectives: Learn two-way prepositions: in, auf, unter, neben, zwischen, vor, hinter, an
Understand the difference between dative (location) and accusative (direction)
Describe where things are and where things are going
units.grammarFocus: Two-way prepositions (Wechselpräpositionen) use dative for location (answering 'Wo?' — Where?) and accusative for direction/movement (answering 'Wohin?' — Where to?). Dative: 'Das Buch liegt auf dem Tisch.' (The book is on the table.) Accusative: 'Ich lege das Buch auf den Tisch.' (I put the book on the table.)
units.culturalNote: Germans are very precise about spatial descriptions. While English might say 'the picture is on the wall,' German distinguishes between 'an der Wand' (on a vertical surface) and 'auf dem Tisch' (on a horizontal surface). This precision reflects the German love of accuracy.

content.sentence: Das Buch liegt auf dem Tisch.
content.translation: The book is lying on the table.

options.text: Die Katze ist unter dem Stuhl.
Die Katze ist unter den Stuhl.

content.transcript: Das Bild hängt an der Wand.

options.text: The picture hangs on the wall.
The picture is under the wall.
The picture is behind the door.

exercises.instruction: Arrange the words to form a sentence.

content.translation: The picture hangs on the wall.

content.context: The lamp stands on the table.

content.question: What does 'vor' mean?

content.rule: Two-way prepositions take dative for location (Wo?) and accusative for direction (Wohin?). Dative: 'auf dem Tisch' (on the table — it's there). Accusative: 'auf den Tisch' (onto the table — moving there). 'dem' = dative (m/n), 'den' = accusative (m).
content.question: 'Ich lege das Buch _____ den Tisch.' (I put the book onto the table.) — This is movement, so which case?
content.explanation: Since 'legen' (to put/lay) describes movement to a new location, it answers 'Wohin?' and requires the accusative: 'auf den Tisch'. If the book were already there (location, 'Wo?'), it would be dative: 'auf dem Tisch'.

content.prompt: Describe where things are in your room:
content.sampleAnswer: Das Buch liegt auf dem Tisch und die Lampe steht neben dem Bett.

units.title: Connecting Sentences
objectives: Learn coordinating conjunctions: und, aber, oder, denn
Learn subordinating conjunctions: weil; and adverbs: dann, deshalb, auch
Understand how conjunctions affect verb position in the sentence
units.grammarFocus: Coordinating conjunctions (und, aber, oder, denn) do not change word order — the verb stays in second position: 'Ich bin müde, aber ich arbeite.' Subordinating conjunctions (weil) send the verb to the END of the clause: 'Ich bleibe zu Hause, weil ich krank bin.' This is one of the most important German grammar rules.
units.culturalNote: Germans highly value logical reasoning. Using 'weil' (because) and 'deshalb' (therefore) to explain your reasoning is appreciated. In formal writing and speech, providing clear justification is considered a sign of thoroughness and respect.

content.sentence: Ich lerne Deutsch, weil ich in Deutschland arbeite.
content.translation: I learn German because I work in Germany.

options.text: Ich bin müde, weil ich viel arbeite.
Ich bin müde, weil ich arbeite viel.
Ich bin müde, aber ich arbeite.

content.transcript: Er ist müde, aber er arbeitet weiter.

options.text: He is tired, but he keeps working.
He is tired because he works.
He is tired and he goes home.

exercises.instruction: Arrange the words to form a sentence.

content.translation: I speak German and English.

content.text: Ich gehe nicht raus, _____ es regnet.
content.context: I don't go outside because it is raining.

content.question: What does 'aber' mean?

content.rule: After 'weil' (because), the conjugated verb moves to the END of the clause: 'Ich lerne Deutsch, weil ich in Berlin wohne.' After 'denn' (because), the verb stays in normal second position: 'Ich lerne Deutsch, denn ich wohne in Berlin.' Both mean the same thing but have different word order.
content.question: Which sentence uses 'weil' with the correct word order?

options.text: Ich bleibe zu Hause, weil ich krank bin.
Ich bleibe zu Hause, weil ich bin krank.
Ich bleibe zu Hause, weil bin ich krank.

content.explanation: After 'weil', the verb goes to the very end of the clause: 'weil ich krank bin' — the subject 'ich' and adjective 'krank' come first, then the verb 'bin' at the end. This is the key rule for subordinating conjunctions in German.

content.prompt: Explain why you are learning German:
content.sampleAnswer: Ich lerne Deutsch, weil ich in Deutschland arbeite.

units.title: Comprehensive A1 Review
objectives: Review all key grammar from Sections 1–5: verbs, articles, cases, negation, and sentence structure
Practice vocabulary from all A1 topics: greetings, family, food, travel, daily life, and grammar
Demonstrate readiness to progress beyond A1 level
units.grammarFocus: This unit reviews all A1 grammar: regular and irregular verb conjugation, separable verbs, adjective usage, comparisons, question formation, negation with nicht/kein, prepositions with dative and accusative, and sentence connectors (und, aber, weil). Mastering these structures is essential for building your own German sentences.
units.culturalNote: Completing A1 is a real achievement! In Germany, the A1 certificate (Goethe-Zertifikat A1) is often required for family reunification visas. You now have the foundation to handle basic conversations, ask for directions, order food, and introduce yourself — skills that make a real difference when visiting German-speaking countries.

content.sentence: Ich heiße Anna, bin 25 Jahre alt und wohne in Berlin.
content.translation: My name is Anna, I am 25 years old and I live in Berlin.

dialogue.speaker: app
dialogue.text: Guten Tag! Wie heißen Sie und woher kommen Sie?
dialogue.translation: Good day! What is your name and where are you from?

options.text: Ich heiße Max und komme aus den USA.
Ich habe Max und bin USA.

content.transcript: Ich stehe um acht Uhr auf und trinke Kaffee.

options.text: I get up at eight o'clock and drink coffee.
I go to school at eight o'clock and eat bread.
I stand outside at eight o'clock and wait.

pairs.source: Das Buch liegt auf dem Tisch.
pairs.target: The book is on the table.

content.prompt: Ich lerne Deutsch, weil ich in Berlin wohne.

options.text: I learn German because I live in Berlin.
I learn German but I live in Berlin.
I learn German and I work in Berlin.

exercises.instruction: Arrange the words to form a sentence.

content.translation: I stay at home because I am sick.

content.text: Mein Bruder ist _____ als meine Schwester.
content.context: My brother is older than my sister.

content.question: Which sentence is grammatically correct?

options.text: Sie steht jeden Morgen früh auf.
Sie steht auf jeden Morgen früh.

content.rule: Review: 'nicht' negates verbs and adjectives. 'kein' negates nouns (replaces ein/eine). Separable prefixes go to the end. 'weil' sends the verb to the end. W-questions put the verb second.
content.question: Which sentence correctly uses 'weil' and 'kein' together?

options.text: Ich bin traurig, weil ich keine Freunde habe.
Ich bin traurig, weil ich habe keine Freunde.
Ich bin traurig, weil ich nicht Freunde habe.

content.explanation: After 'weil', the verb 'habe' goes to the end: 'weil ich keine Freunde habe'. 'Keine' (not 'nicht') is correct because it negates the noun 'Freunde' (friends). The verb must be the last element in a 'weil' clause.

content.prompt: Introduce yourself fully — name, age, where you live, what you do, and something you like:
content.sampleAnswer: Ich heiße Max, bin 22 Jahre alt und wohne in Berlin. Ich lerne gern Deutsch.

checkpoint.title: Section 5 Checkpoint: Building Sentences
checkpoint.description: Test your knowledge of German grammar fundamentals — verb conjugation, negation, prepositions, and sentence connectors.

exercises.instruction: Match each grammar concept to the correct example.

content.question: Which sentence has correct word order after 'weil'?

options.text: Ich bleibe zu Hause, weil ich krank bin.
Ich bleibe zu Hause, weil ich bin krank.
Ich bleibe zu Hause, weil bin ich krank.

content.prompt: Write three sentences: say where you live, what you do, and why you learn German.
content.text: Erzähl mir von dir und deinem Deutsch!
content.sampleAnswer: Ich wohne in Berlin und arbeite als Lehrer. Ich lerne Deutsch, weil ich in Deutschland lebe.
